efficacyPairs.questionText: Which three-letter word completes this longer word? CH _ _ _ ER
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: Look at **CH _ _ SE** — the middle is missing! Read the start of the word, then the end. Think of words that begin and end this way — the answer must fit in the middle like a sandwich!
lessonLearningGoal: The sandwich method: use the start AND end of a word to work out what's in the middle!
How knowing the outside letters helps you fill in the gap — like a word sandwich

efficacyPairs.questionText: Which single letter completes this word? B _ AUTY

lessonLearningGoal: A brilliant shortcut: when letters are missing, try the vowels (A, E, I, O, U) first!
Why vowels are often the missing piece and how testing them cracks the word in seconds

efficacyPairs.questionText: Which single letter completes this word? SC _ ENCE

Why vowels are often the missing piece and how testing them cracks the word in seconds

efficacyPairs.questionText: Which single letter completes this word? _ NOWLEDGE
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: Look at **_ A _ E**. When consonants (letters like B, C, D, F, G...) are missing, the vowels you CAN see tell you what the word sounds like. Sound it out!
lessonLearningGoal: Flipping it around: sometimes the missing letters are consonants, not vowels!
A clever trick — looking at the vowels you CAN see helps you work out what's missing

efficacyPairs.questionText: Which single letter completes this word? RHYTH _
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: Look at **FORE _ _ _** — the ending is missing! Read the start and think of common English endings: -DOM, -FUL, -MENT, -NESS, -ING, -TION.
lessonLearningGoal: How to solve words with missing letters at the end — common endings are your secret weapon!
Why knowing endings like ING, TION, LY and ED gives you a massive head start

efficacyPairs.questionText: Which letter completes this word? R H _ T H M

efficacyPairs.questionText: Which three-letter word goes in front to make a longer word? ( _ _ _ ) LING

efficacyPairs.questionText:
Which three-letter word completes this longer word?

F ( _ _ _ ) YARD
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: Look at **CH _ _ SE** — the middle is missing! Read the start of the word, then the end. Think of words that begin and end this way — the answer must fit in the middle like a sandwich!
lessonLearningGoal: The sandwich method: use the start AND end of a word to work out what's in the middle!
How knowing the outside letters helps you fill in the gap — like a word sandwich
